title: Isospectrality and Operator Complexity
authors: Pradip Kattel, Yicheng Tang, Natan Andrei
abstract: We study a pair of exactly solvable, isospectral fermion chains, one strongly interacting and one quadratic, that nevertheless display remarkably different phase structures and operator dynamics. A nonlocal nonlinear unitary transformation maps one onto the other while preserving the entire many-body spectrum and converting local fermion operators into extended many-body strings. Thus, operators that evolve within a closed linear subspace in the quadratic model become interacting operators that generate increasingly higher-body terms and exhibit asymptotic Lanczos growth $b_n\propto\sqrt n$. Despite their identical spectra, the two models realize distinct phases and sharply different notions of operator complexity. Our results demonstrate that free many-body spectra and interacting operator dynamics are fundamentally compatible.
summary: This work explores whether two different interacting quantum models can be distinguished if they share the exact same many-body spectrum. By comparing an interacting chain to its quadratic dual, the authors show that despite spectral identity, the models exhibit distinct phase structures and fundamentally different operator growth rates. This proves that energy eigenvalues alone are insufficient to characterize all aspects of a complex quantum system.
main_problem: To investigate whether identical many-body spectra (isospectrality) are sufficient to determine all physical properties, such as phase structure or operator complexity.
main_result: The study shows that two Hamiltonians sharing the same spectrum can exhibit distinct phases and vastly different operator dynamics, demonstrating that spectral equivalence does not constrain all physical observables.
method: Theoretical analysis involving nonlocal unitary transformations to relate an interacting Hamiltonian to a quadratic one, followed by calculating diagnostics like Out-of-Time-Order Correlators (OTOCs) and Krylov complexity growth rates.
model_or_system: A pair of exactly solvable fermion chains: one strongly interacting ($	ext{H}_{	ext{int}}$) and its dual, quadratic/free-fermion counterpart ($	ext{H}_{	ext{ff}}(\lambda)$). The models are related by a nonlocal unitary transformation.
key_observables: Phase structure (e.g., CDW order), operator growth rates (Lanczos coefficients $b_n \propto \sqrt{n}$ vs. bounded), and OTOCs.
important_parameters: Interaction strength $\lambda$, which controls the specific phase realized in the system.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mapping local operators to extended Majorana strings via a nonlocal unitary transformation, allowing for exact computation of correlators using Pfaffians.
figures_summary: Figures compare spectra (confirming equivalence), density correlations diagnosing different phases ($\lambda$ dependence), OTOCs showing shared light cones but differing internal structure, and Lanczos coefficients demonstrating $\sqrt{n}$ growth in the interacting model.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the existence of two isospectral models, analyzes their spectral agreement, then contrasts their phase structures and operator dynamics using advanced diagnostics like Krylov complexity to prove that spectra alone are insufficient for full physical characterization.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful theoretical tool—the concept of 'operator complexity' beyond mere energy eigenvalues—to distinguish between physically distinct quantum phases even when the underlying Hamiltonians share identical ground state energies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Many-body spectra of the quadratic Hamiltonian Hff(λ) (blue circles) and the interacting Hamiltonian Hint(λ) (red crosses) for a chain of length N = 12 at λ = 0.5, 1, 1.5. The complete level-by-level agreement demonstrates the exact unitary equivalence between the interacting nearest-neighbor fermion chain Eq. (1) and the quadratic nearest-neighbor Hamiltonian Eq. (3). Inset: spectral differences ∆Ek = E(int) k −E(ff) k vanish to numerical precision.
FIG. 2. Ground-state density correlations Cij = � ni −1
FIG. 3. Infinite-temperature OTOCs of centered density op- erators Wj = nj −1

FIG. 4. Lanczos coefficients bn from the Liouvillian Krylov recursion with a local Majorana seed, computed numerically for a chain of length N = 100 up to n = 65 for various values of λ. To suppress short-range numerical fluctuations, the raw sequence is smoothed using a Savitzky–Golay filter (window size 11, quadratic polynomial) [24]. The resulting coefficients are consistent with bn ∝√n over the accessible Krylov depths, demonstrating distinct operator complexity despite exact isospectrality.
